facts.content: We set the database connection pool size to 20 in production after the incident.

chunks.content: A database connection pool keeps open database connections so the application reuses a connection instead of opening a new database connection each time in production.
Connection pool sizing for a database depends on the database server connection limit and the production workload on the database connection pool.
The database connection pool exposes connection pool metrics: active connections, idle connections, and connection pool wait time under production database load.
Tuning a database connection pool in production measures connection acquisition latency against the database connection limit and the connection pool size.
When the database connection pool is exhausted the application blocks waiting for a free database connection, hurting production throughput on the database.
A database connection pool size that is too large overwhelms the database server with more production connections than the database can serve.

facts.content: We keep application logs for 30 days in hot storage and archive logs for one year.

chunks.content: A log retention window is how long log data is kept before deletion; log retention is driven by cost and the retention window policy.
Hot log storage is costly so teams shorten the hot log retention window in days and archive older logs past the retention window.
Compliance can mandate a minimum log retention window in days or years for audit logs and their retention window.
Log retention tooling enforces the retention window by deleting log files older than the configured retention window in days.
A longer log retention window helps forensics but grows the log index over the retention window period in days.
Structured logs compress well, lowering the cost of a long log retention window across the retention days.

facts.content: PaymentService runs three replicas behind the gateway and autoscales on CPU.

chunks.content: A deployment status check reports whether replicas are healthy behind a gateway in a microservice architecture.
Deployment status dashboards show replica counts, gateway routing, and autoscaling decisions for many services.
Service deployment status depends on replica readiness probes and gateway health checks across the fleet.
Autoscaling deployment status reflects how replicas scale on CPU behind the gateway under load.
A deployment status rollup aggregates replica health and gateway error rates per service deployment.
Deployment status alerts fire when replica count drops below the gateway's minimum across deployments.

facts.content: OrderQueue is capped at 10000 messages with a dead-letter queue after five retries.

chunks.content: A backlog configuration sets how many messages a queue holds before it applies backpressure to producers.
Queue backlog configuration interacts with consumer throughput; a large backlog hides slow consumers.
Backlog configuration includes message cap, retention, and dead-letter handling for a message queue.
Monitoring queue backlog configuration means tracking depth, age, and the dead-letter rate of the queue.
A backlog configuration with no cap risks unbounded queue growth and memory pressure on the broker.
Backlog configuration tuning balances queue throughput against message latency under load.

facts.content: Metadata is kept in memory with a 512 MB ceiling and a least-recently-used drop rule.

chunks.content: A caching strategy decides what to cache and for how long; a caching strategy trades freshness for speed.
A read-through caching strategy loads on miss; a write-through caching strategy updates the cache on write.
Choosing a caching strategy depends on the access pattern; a caching strategy with high locality benefits most.
A caching strategy must handle invalidation; a poor caching strategy serves stale data to clients.
A tiered caching strategy layers a local cache under a shared cache as part of the caching strategy.
A caching strategy is measured by hit rate; a good caching strategy raises the hit rate of the cache.

facts.content: Each consumer is capped at 100 calls a minute via a token bucket keyed on the access key.

chunks.content: A rate limiting approach caps request volume to protect a service; a rate limiting approach can be local or distributed.
A common rate limiting approach is the leaky bucket; another rate limiting approach smooths bursts over a window.
A rate limiting approach needs a stable identifier so the rate limiting approach applies to the right caller.
A distributed rate limiting approach shares counters so every node enforces the same rate limiting approach.
Choosing a rate limiting approach balances fairness against burst tolerance in the rate limiting approach.
A rate limiting approach that is too strict frustrates callers; a lax rate limiting approach invites abuse.
